1: tĕhillāh lĕdāwid ʾărômimĕkā ʾĕlôhay hammĕlek ʾăbārĕkāh šimĕkā lĕʿôlām wāʿed — "a praise of David: I will exalt you, my God and King, and bless your name forever and ever" — the superscription tĕhillāh lĕdāwid (a praise of David) — uniquely among the Psalms, Ps 145 is the only one explicitly titled a tĕhillāh (praise, from hālal). In rabbinic Judaism this became the Ashrei psalm, recited three times daily. Ps 145 is a Hebrew acrostic — each verse begins with successive letters (aleph through taw, with the nun letter missing in the MT — probably due to Amos 5:2's use of the nāpĕlāh [fallen] for Israel). ʾĕlôhay hammĕlek (my God and King) — dual title: relational (ʾĕlôhay, my God) and royal (mĕlek, king). YHWH is both covenant partner and sovereign.
2: bĕkhol-yôm ʾăbārĕkekā waʾăhallĕlāh šimĕkā lĕʿôlām wāʿed — "every day I will bless you and praise your name forever and ever" — bĕkhol-yôm (every day) — the daily rhythm of blessing: not annual or occasional but quotidian. The temporal markers expand: bĕkhol-yôm (every day) + lĕʿôlām wāʿed (forever and ever). Praise as the fundamental orientation of each day within an eternal horizon. The three-times-daily recitation in Judaism embodies precisely this daily rhythm.
3: gādôl yhwh ûmĕhullāl mĕʾōd wĕligdullātô ʾên ḥēqer — "the LORD is great and highly to be praised; his greatness no one can search out" — gādôl yhwh ûmĕhullāl mĕʾōd (the LORD is great and greatly to be praised) — the pairing of greatness and praise-worthiness: the magnitude of YHWH is the very ground of praise. ligdullātô ʾên ḥēqer (his greatness has no searching out/fathoming) — ḥēqer (searching, investigation, from ḥāqar, to search out, examine) — YHWH's greatness is incomprehensible, beyond the reach of investigation. The Psalter's "great" psalms make this claim repeatedly (Ps 48:1; 95:3).
4: dôr lĕdôr yĕšabbaḥ maʿăśêkā ûgĕbûrōtêkā yaggîdû — "one generation will commend your works to the next and will tell of your mighty deeds" — dôr lĕdôr (generation to generation) — the intergenerational transmission of praise is both historical continuity and theological necessity: the works of YHWH must be passed on, or they are forgotten. yĕšabbaḥ maʿăśêkā (they will laud/praise your works, from šābaḥ, to praise, commend — the same verb as Ps 117:1) — the emphasis on works and deeds (maʿăśêkā, gĕbûrôt) grounds the praise in history, not merely in abstract attribute.
5: hădar kĕbôd hôdĕkā wĕdibrê nipĕlĕʾōtêkā ʾāśîḥāh — "I will speak of the splendor of your glorious majesty and of your wonderful deeds" — hădār kĕbôd hôd (splendor, glory, majesty) — three overlapping words for divine magnificence; the Psalter uses such pileups to gesture toward what language cannot fully capture. nipĕlĕʾōtêkā (your wonderful deeds, from pālaʾ, to be extraordinary, beyond what is possible) — the pĕlāʾîm (wonders) are by definition things that transcend human capacity and expectation.
6: wĕʿezûz nôrĕʾōtêkā yōʾmērû ûgĕdullātĕkā ʾăsapĕrennāh — "people will tell of the power of your awe-inspiring acts, and I will declare your greatness" — nôrĕʾōtêkā (your awesome/fearsome acts, Niphal participle of yārēʾ, to fear) — divine actions that inspire awe and fear. The structure alternates between communal proclamation ("they will say," "people will tell") and personal testimony ("I will speak," "I will declare") — the communal and individual voices of praise interwoven throughout Ps 145.
7: zēker rab-ṭûbĕkā yabbîʿû wĕṣidqātĕkā yĕrannēnû — "they will overflow with praise for your great goodness and sing aloud of your righteousness" — yabbîʿû (they will pour out, bubble up, gush, from nābaʿ, to spring up, bubble forth) — praise as a spring that wells up and overflows, not a managed flow. rab-ṭûbĕkā (the abundance of your goodness) — ṭûb (goodness, beauty, prosperity) combined with rab (abundance, greatness). The pairing of ṭûb (goodness) and ṣĕdāqāh (righteousness) is characteristic of the Psalter's theology: YHWH's goodness is not separate from his right-ordering of all things.
8: ḥannûn wĕraḥûm yhwh ʾerek ʾappayim ûgĕdal-ḥāsed — "the LORD is gracious and compassionate, slow to anger and great in steadfast love" — verbatim reproduction of Exod 34:6, the divine self-disclosure at Sinai after the golden calf. This verse is the most-quoted passage in the entire Psalter (Ps 86:15; 103:8; 111:4; 112:4; 116:5; Neh 9:17; Jon 4:2; Joel 2:13) — each time it appears it carries the full weight of the Sinai revelation. In context, Ps 145 is specifically citing this tradition: YHWH's rule as king (vv1-2) is characterized by the covenant attributes of grace, compassion, patience, and abundant love.
9: ṭôb-yhwh lakkōl wĕraḥămāyw ʿal-kol-maʿăśāyw — "the LORD is good to all; his compassion extends over all he has made" — ṭôb-yhwh lakkōl (the LORD is good to all/everything) — the universal scope is striking: not just to Israel, not just to humans, but to all. wĕraḥămāyw ʿal-kol-maʿăśāyw (his compassion/tender mercies over all his works) — raḥămîm (plural of reḥem, womb-compassion) extends over the entire created order. This verse is the theological ground of the later description (v15-16) of YHWH feeding all living things.
10: yôdûkā yhwh kol-maʿăśêkā waḥăsîdêkā yĕbārĕkûkāh — "all your works will praise you, LORD, and your devoted ones will bless you" — the created order as the sphere of universal praise (anticipated in v9's universal compassion). kol-maʿăśêkā (all your works) — all creation as praise-community. ḥăsîdêkā (your devoted/faithful ones, from ḥesed) — within the universal praise-community, Israel's ḥăsîdîm are the explicit speaking/blessing subgroup. The distinction: creation praises implicitly by its existence; the covenant people bless explicitly by their speech.
11: kĕbôd malkûtĕkā yōʾmērû ûgĕbûrātĕkā yĕdabbērû — "they will speak of the glory of your kingdom and tell of your power" — the kingdom (malkût) theme begins here and runs through v13. kĕbôd malkût (the glory of the kingdom) — both YHWH's intrinsic glory and the manifestation of that glory in his royal rule. gĕbûrāh (power, strength, might) — the power exercised in royal action.
12: lĕhôdîaʿ libnê hāʾādām gĕbûrōtāyw ûkĕbôd hădār malkûtô — "to make known to all humanity your mighty deeds and the majestic glory of your kingdom" — libnê hāʾādām (to the sons of Adam/humanity) — the witness extends beyond Israel to all human beings; the ḥăsîdîm's testimony serves a universal communicative purpose. The psalmist's praise is not merely self-edifying but outward-facing: making known (hôdîaʿ, Hiphil of yādaʿ) the nature of YHWH's kingdom to those who do not yet know it.
13: malkûtĕkā malkût kol-ʿōlāmîm ûmemšaltĕkā bĕkhol-dôr wādôr — "your kingdom is an everlasting kingdom, and your dominion endures through all generations" — malkût kol-ʿōlāmîm (kingdom of all ages, lit. "kingship of all eternities") — the plural ʿōlāmîm emphasizes the absolute temporal scope. ûmemšaltĕkā (your dominion, from māšal, to rule) — royal authority persisting through every generation. This verse is cited in the doxology of the Lord's Prayer ("thine is the kingdom" — though the doxology is a later addition) and directly echoed in Dan 4:3,34 (even the Gentile king Nebuchadnezzar confesses YHWH's eternal kingdom).
14: sômēk yhwh lĕkhol-hannōpĕlîm wĕzôqēp lĕkhol-hakkĕpûpîm — "the LORD upholds all who are falling and lifts up all who are bowed down" — sômēk (the one who supports, upholds, from sāmak, to lean on, support, prop — the same root as Ps 111:8, 112:8) — YHWH as the structural support for those who would otherwise fall. hannōpĕlîm (those who are falling, from nāpal, to fall) — catching them in the act of falling. hakkĕpûpîm (those who are bowed down, bent double, from kāpap, to bend, bow) — those doubled over under a load. The verse is both physical (the fallen, the stooped) and social/spiritual (the crushed, the humbled).
15: ʿênê-khōl ʾēlêkā yĕśabbērû wĕʾattāh nôtēn-lāhem ʾet-ʾoklām bĕʿittô — "the eyes of all look to you, and you give them their food in due season" — ʿênê-khōl ʾēlêkā yĕśabbērû (the eyes of all are hoping toward you) — śābar (to wait, hope, expect) — the same expectancy as Ps 130:5's qāwāh. All creation is in a posture of expectant looking. bĕʿittô (in its time, in due season) — YHWH's provision is timed: not random or capricious but ordered to the rhythm of need.
16: pôtēaḥ ʾet-yādĕkā ûmaśbîaʿ lĕkhol-ḥay rāṣôn — "you open your hand and satisfy the desire of every living thing" — pôtēaḥ ʾet-yādĕkā (you open your hand) — the open hand of YHWH as the gesture of giving; the image condenses the entire theology of divine provision. ûmaśbîaʿ lĕkhol-ḥay rāṣôn (and satisfy the desire of every living thing) — rāṣôn (desire, pleasure, goodwill, delight) — not merely the physical need but the full desire of the creature. Every living thing (kol-ḥay) — the scope is universal: not just humans, not just Israel, but all life.
17: ṣaddîq yhwh bĕkhol-dĕrākhāyw wĕḥāsîd bĕkhol-maʿăśāyw — "the LORD is righteous in all his ways and faithful/loving in all his works" — ṣaddîq (righteous) + ḥāsîd (faithful, devoted, characterized by ḥesed) — the two attributes cover YHWH's moral alignment (righteousness) and his relational fidelity (ḥesed). bĕkhol-dĕrākhāyw (in all his ways) + bĕkhol-maʿăśāyw (in all his works) — the paired "all" is emphatic: no exception to righteousness or fidelity in any of YHWH's paths or acts.
18: qārôb yhwh lĕkhol-qōrĕʾāyw lĕkhol ʾăšer yiqrāʾûhû beʾĕmet — "the LORD is near to all who call on him, to all who call on him in sincerity/truth" — qārôb (near, Qal adjective from qārab, to draw near) — YHWH's nearness is the counter to his transcendence. The cosmic king (vv11-13) who governs all ages is also qārôb (near) to the individual who calls. beʾĕmet (in truth, sincerity, faithfulness) — the qualifier that the calling must be genuine. ʾĕmet-shaped prayer is prayer aligned with reality, not manipulation.
19: rĕṣôn-yĕrēʾāyw yaʿăśeh wĕʾet-šawʿātām yišmaʿ wĕyôšîʿēm — "he fulfills the desire of those who fear him; he hears their cry and saves them" — rĕṣôn-yĕrēʾāyw yaʿăśeh (the desire of those who fear him he fulfills) — the same rāṣôn (desire) as v16 where all living things' desires are satisfied; now the specific sub-community of the God-fearers receives particular attention. Fear of the LORD is the channel through which general divine goodness (vv9,16) flows specifically to those in relationship with him.
20: šômēr yhwh ʾet-kol-ʾōhăbāyw wĕʾēt kol-hārĕšāʿîm yašmîd — "the LORD guards all who love him, but he will destroy all the wicked" — the final antithesis in the acrostic (missing only the nun verse). ʾōhăbāyw (those who love him) — love reciprocating love; the God who showed ḥesed is loved by those who experience it. yašmîd (will destroy, from šāmad, to annihilate, destroy completely) — the same fate as the enemies of Israel under the ban. The Psalter does not sentimentalize divine goodness: the open hand of v16 and the destroying word of v20 belong to the same God.
21: tĕhillat yhwh yĕdabbēr-pî wîbārēk kol-bāśār šēm qodšô lĕʿôlām wāʿed — "my mouth will speak the praise of the LORD, and let all living flesh bless his holy name forever and ever" — the psalm closes by circling back to v1's individual pledge ("I will bless your name forever and ever") but expanding it to kol-bāśār (all flesh, all living creatures — the broadest possible biological category). The move from "I will exalt you" (v1) to "let all flesh bless" (v21) traces the psalm's theological arc: individual praise becomes universal praise. The acrostic that covers the whole Hebrew alphabet mirrors the universal scope of the praise content.
